1.premise: In American English, "white" as a modifier signals superiority or power in every context.
"White House" could be reframed as proof that only the "white" version of something holds real power.

2.premise: "White collar" could be reframed as evidence that calling work "white" makes it superior and better paid.

3.premise: "White lives" can mean racially white people or lives with good intentions, making the slogan a compliment.

4.premise: Hosting all-white parties implies Diddy embraces whiteness, but reveals he wasn't white enough for a White House pardon.

lines.text: Oh, you know, as I'm learning more about this country in English,
lines.label: setup

lines.text: I realize everything's better when it's white.
lines.label: setup

lines.text: For example, a normal person just lives in the house,
lines.label: setup

lines.text: but the most powerful person lives in the white house.
lines.label: punchline

lines.text: White-collar jobs are better than blue-collar jobs.
lines.label: setup

lines.text: They're more intellectual and pay more.
lines.label: punchline

lines.text: White lives are better than other lives
lines.label: setup

lines.text: because they come from good intentions.
lines.label: punchline

lines.text: I'll be some sort of white.
lines.label: tag

lines.text: Even Diddy knows white is better.
lines.label: setup

lines.text: Diddy used to host white parties in Hollywood.
lines.label: setup

lines.text: Best parties you can ever find.
lines.label: setup

lines.text: You can only wear white in that party, right?
lines.label: setup

lines.text: They got A-list celebrities, unlimited baby oil.
lines.label: setup

lines.text: And guess what? When he's arrested,
lines.label: setup

lines.text: not white enough to get pardoned by the White House.
lines.label: punchline
